did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning (convolutional neural networks) for melanoma detection. Specifically, it implemented a multi-network system based on decision fusion using architectures such as DarkNet-53, DenseNet-201, GoogLeNet, Inception-V3, InceptionResNet-V2, ResNet-50, and ResNet-101. These models were trained on the ISIC 2020 and DermIS databases to classify melanoma versus non-melanoma skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the implementation of a multi-network system based on decision fusion using specific CNN architectures (DarkNet-53, DenseNet-201, etc.) trained on the ISIC 2020 and DermIS databases. It includes a methodology section detailing data preprocessing, model training, and validation, followed by a results section reporting performance metrics (F1 score, confusion matrices) and comparing the proposed system against other applications. The structure follows standard research article format with Introduction, Materials and Methods, Results, Discussion, and Conclusion.

title: Performance Improvement of Melanoma Detection Using a Multi-Network System Based on Decision Fusion
brief_summary: This study addresses the critical need for early melanoma detection by developing a multi-network system based on decision fusion using seven convolutional neural network architectures: DarkNet-53, DenseNet-201, GoogLeNet, Inception-V3, InceptionResNet-V2, ResNet-50, and ResNet-101. The models were trained and evaluated on two distinct dermatological datasets, ISIC 2020 and DermIS, to assess adaptability across varying image sources. The proposed system's performance was rigorously compared against four existing commercial applications, including Google Teachable Machine, Microsoft Azure Machine Learning, Google Vertex AI, and Salesforce Einstein Vision, using accuracy and F1 score metrics. The results demonstrated that the proposed decision fusion classifier achieved superior performance, attaining an accuracy of 0.9550 and an F1 score of 0.9550 on the DermIS dataset, and 0.9350 accuracy and F1 score on the ISIC dataset. These findings confirm the system's promising potential for integration into Electronic Health Record platforms to improve patient prognosis and reduce diagnostic errors.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for detection. The study classifies images as either melanoma or non-melanoma. The ISIC 2020 dataset subset used contained melanoma images (Figure 3), and the DermIS database subset contained 500 images portraying melanoma cases.

The negative class in the binary classification task. The ISIC 2020 dataset subset used contained non-melanoma images (Figure 2). The DermIS database subset contained 500 images depicting healthy skin conditions, which served as the non-melanoma comparator.

all_task_types.details: The paper implements a multi-network system for binary classification of skin lesions into 'Melanoma' (M) and 'Non-Melanoma' (NM) classes. The models output binary decisions (0 or 1) and confidence scores (probabilities) which are fused via decision fusion. Performance is evaluated using accuracy and F1 score on the ISIC 2020 and DermIS datasets.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2020
datasets_used.description: A subset of the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2020 database, which is a publicly available collection of dermoscopic skin lesion images sourced from various global institutions involving over 2000 patients. The study used a specific subset of 1000 images from this database.

use.training: Used for training the proposed CNN architectures (700 images).
use.validation: Used for validation during training (300 images).
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed system (200 images).

datasets_used.name: DermIS
datasets_used.description: A comprehensive dermatological repository available on the internet, conducted in conjunction with the Department of Socio-Clinical Medicine at the University of Heidelberg and the Department of Dermatology at the University of Erlangen. The study utilized the entire collection of 1000 images from this database, which includes images of melanoma cases and healthy skin conditions.

use.training: Used for training the proposed CNN architectures (700 images).
use.validation: Used for validation during training (300 images).
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed system (200 images).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were based on descriptive metrics such as accuracy, F1 score, and confusion matrices.

ISIC 2020.notes: Training configuration: 6 epochs, learning rate 0.0001, 276 max iterations. Subset of 1000 images used (700 training, 300 validation, 200 testing).

DermIS.notes: Training configuration: 6 epochs, learning rate 0.0001, 276 max iterations. Subset of 1000 images used (700 training, 300 validation, 200 testing).

ISIC 2020 (Individual CNN Testing).notes: DarkNet-53 stood out with the highest F1 score among the listed models for this dataset. GoogLeNet achieved 0.8898.

DermIS (Individual CNN Testing).notes: GoogLeNet achieved the highest F1 score of 0.8899. DarkNet-53, ResNet-50, and DenseNet-201 attained F1 scores of 0.804.

ISIC 2020 (Automated Applications).notes: Microsoft Azure Machine Learning achieved the most remarkable accuracy and F1 score. Google Vertex AI recorded the highest count of false positives.

DermIS (Automated Applications).notes: Google Vertex AI observed the highest accuracy. Google Teachable Machine yielded the highest number of false positives.

ISIC 2020 (Proposed Decision Fusion Syst.notes: The proposed system yielded superior results compared to the four commercial applications. It generated a greater number of False Positives than False Negatives.

DermIS (Proposed Decision Fusion System).notes: The proposed system yielded superior results compared to the four commercial applications. It generated a greater number of False Positives than False Negatives.

architecture_used.main_model: Multi-network system based on decision fusion using seven Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs): DarkNet-53, DenseNet-201, GoogLeNet, Inception-V3, InceptionResNet-V2, ResNet-50, ResNet-101, and Xception.
architecture_used.pretraining: All CNN models were pre-trained on the ImageNet database.
architecture_used.input_size: Images were pre-processed by eliminating hair and resizing them to fit the input of the CNNs. Specific input dimensions are not reported.

architecture_details.fusion_method: Decision fusion classifier combining outputs from the individual CNNs.

classification_or_output_head.details: Outputs binary decisions (0 or 1) and confidence scores (probabilities) which are fused via decision fusion.

preprocessing_techniques: Hair artifact removal using the DullRazor algorithm (morphological closing, bilinear interpolation, median filtering), sometimes applied twice for ISIC 2020 images with thick hairs or high noise
Image resizing to specific input dimensions for each CNN architecture (e.g., 256 × 256 × 3 pixels for GoogLeNet) with a 1:1 aspect ratio
Conversion of color images to grayscale
conclusions: The proposed multi-network system based on decision fusion achieved superior performance compared to four commercial applications (Google Teachable Machine, Microsoft Azure Machine Learning, Google Vertex AI, and Salesforce Einstein Vision) and individual CNN architectures.
On the DermIS dataset, the proposed decision fusion classifier achieved an accuracy and F1 score of 0.9550.
On the ISIC 2020 dataset, the proposed decision fusion classifier achieved an accuracy and F1 score of 0.9350.
Among individual CNN architectures, GoogLeNet achieved the highest F1 score on the DermIS dataset (0.8899), while DarkNet-53 achieved the highest F1 score on the ISIC 2020 dataset (0.8898).
The system demonstrated adaptability across different image sources, with performance varying between the ISIC 2020 and DermIS databases, attributed to differences in image quality and noise levels.

The study confirms the promising potential of the proposed system for integration into Electronic Health Record (EHR) platforms to improve patient prognosis, reduce diagnostic errors, and support medical professionals in swift diagnosis.
The proposed system generated a greater number of false positives than false negatives on both datasets.
important_remarks: The study uses a very small subset of the ISIC 2020 dataset (1000 images) and the entire DermIS database (1000 images), which limits the generalizability of the results compared to full-scale benchmarks.

The training dataset is highly imbalanced with respect to the original source populations, as the DermIS subset consists of exactly 500 melanoma and 500 non-melanoma images, potentially introducing selection bias.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the performance differences between the proposed system and commercial applications.

The proposed system generated more false positives than false negatives on both datasets, which is a critical clinical caveat for melanoma detection where false negatives are typically more dangerous.

The comparison with commercial applications (Google Teachable Machine, Azure, etc.) may not be fully fair as these platforms often have access to larger, proprietary training data not available to the custom models.
The study lacks external validation on independent datasets beyond ISIC 2020 and DermIS, limiting evidence of robustness across different clinical settings.
The paper does not report the number of parameters or computational complexity of the proposed multi-network fusion system, making it difficult to assess deployment feasibility.
Data leakage risks are present if the preprocessing steps (like DullRazor) were applied before splitting the data, although the text implies standard train/validation/test splits.
